rulings.Case Title: BURNS TOMMY et al VS. STATE OF CALIFORNIA et al
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, March 16, 2010, line 1, DEFENDANT STATE OF CALIFORNIA DEMURRER TO THIRD AMENDED COMPLAINT. Off Calendar, matter assigned to Judge Kramer. =(302/CWW)

rulings.Case Title: DEBRA EDGERLY VS. PHILIP KIELY et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Strike Pltf'S First Amended Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, March 16, 2010, line 2, DEFENDANTS PHILIP KIELY, FILLMORE PLAZA APARTMENTS Motion To Strike Plaintiff's First Amended Complaint. Motion to strike granted, without leave to amend. Plaintiff failed to allege facts that show defendants acted with malice and oppression, and that their conduct was despicable. (Lee Lackner vs. North (2006) 135 Cal.App. 4th 1188.) Plaintiff was provided a previous opportunity to amend punitive damages allegation and was not able to do so. No request to amend contained in oppositon. No reasonable probability that defect can be cured by amendment. =(302/CWW)

rulings.Case Title: KHADIJAH GHAFUR VS. WALT DISNEY COMPANY/ABC NEWS, INC. AND AFFILIATES et al (Provide Access)
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion By Specially Appearing Defts The Walt Disney Company Et Al: 1) To Require Ghafure To Post Security As A Condition Of Proceeding, Or, Alternatively; 2) To Declare Ghafur A Vexatious Litigant And Require Her To Post Security As A Condition Of Proceeding
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, March 16, 2010, line 4, DEFENDANT THE WALT DISNEY COMPANY, AMERICAN BROADCASTING COMPANIES, INC., KGO TELEVISION, INC., KFSN TELEVISION, LLC, Motion By Specially Appearing Defendants The Walt Disney Company et al, 1) To Require Ghafure To Post Security as a Condition of Proceeding, or, Alternatively; 2) To Declare Ghafur a Vexatious Litigant and Require Her To Post Security as a Condition of Proceeding. Motion granted. Plaintiff has been declared a vexatious litigant in Los Angeles County Superior Court Case Number EC050452. Plaintiff must post a security in the amount of $10,000 on or before March 30, 2010 as a condition of proceeding with this lawsuit in San Francisco Superior Court Case Number CGC-09-493222. If security is not timely posted, this action will be dismissed. Hearing Required. =(302/CWW)

rulings.Case Title: JOSEPH BANDIERA VS. DUNCAN EASTWOOD et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff'S Order To Show Cause To Defendant Duncan Eastwood Re: Preliminary Injunction
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, March 16, 2010, line 5, Plaintiff's Order To Show Cause To Defendant Duncan Eastwood Re: Preliminary Injunction. Motion is denied. Plaintiff fails to show that the legal remedy is inadequate. The suit involves monetary damages that are readily ascertainable and would adequately compensate plaintiff if he prevails. Inadequate showing of irreparable, imminent harm absent the injunction. No detailed financial information provided to support allegations. Plaintiff does not demonstrate a high probability of success at trial, in light of the opposing evidence and allegations. It is not clear that balance of equities favor plaintiff, and both parties allege improper conduct on the part of the other. =(302/CWW)

rulings.Case Title: HOPOLA INC A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION DBA A BODY OF VS. CALIFORNIA DEPARTMENT OF INDUSTRIAL DIVISION OF et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion For Writ Of Administrative Mandate
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, March 16, 2010, line 9, PETITIONER HOPOLA INC. Motion For Writ Of Administrative Mandate. Motion denied. Failure to object to evidence at an administrative hearing operates as a waiver. See Tennant vs. Civil Service Comm. (1946) 77 Cal.App. 2nd 489, 498. Petitioner did not object to documents at the hearing and acted as though they were authentic. Petitioner's representative conceded that some of the contract is correct (RT 26). Hearing without objection is sufficient to support a finding in the absence of contrary evidence. Frudden Enters vs. ALRB (1984) 153 Cal.App. 3rd 262. The ALJ did not rely solely on hearsay to render her decision. Sullivan made numerous admissions at the hearing, including that she switched her business model from employer/employee to independent contractor (RT 13-14). Substantial evidence supports the ALJ's decision. Petitioner admitted it bought all of the equipment at the studio. The business cards and conditions in the contract show that petitioner exerted a high degree of control over the trainers. No arguments were raised regarding the reduction of the penalty under Labor Code section 226.3 or the recording of the hearing, at the hearing. Therefore, those issues are waived. =(302/CWW)
